sections.heading: Oath of confidentiality
sections.title: Confidentiality oath and duty
sections.summary: The Director-General and Centre staff must take a confidentiality oath before starting duties and maintain confidentiality of all matters encountered during employment. This obligation applies to all Centre personnel and covers both active and post-employment periods.

requirements.text: Director-General and Centre staff must take confidentiality oath before starting duties
Centre staff must maintain confidentiality of all matters encountered during employment

sections.heading: Books of accounts, records, audit and reports
sections.title: Centre's accounting and audit requirements
sections.summary: The Centre must maintain proper books of accounts and records of income, expenditure, assets, and liabilities. It must submit financial statements to the Controller and Auditor-General within three months of financial year closure. The Centre's accounts must be audited per the Public Audit Act, 2003.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires Centre to maintain financial records and undergo audits, relevant for DTIA when assessing internal financial data handling.

requirements.text: The Centre must maintain proper books of accounts and records of income, expenditure, assets, and liabilities
The Centre must submit financial statements to the Controller and Auditor-General within three months of financial year closure
The Centre's accounts must be audited and reported upon in accordance with the Public Audit Act (2003)

sections.source_quote: 43. (1) The Centre shall cause to be kept proper books of accounts and records of accounts of the income, expenditure, assets and liabilities of the Centre. (2) The Centre shall within three months of the closure of the financial year submit to the Controller and Auditor-General— (a) a statement of income and expenditure during that period; (b) a statement of the assets and liabilities of the Centre on the last day of that year. (3) The accounts of the Centre shall be audited and reported upon in accordance with the Public Audit Act (2003).

sections.heading: Application of reporting obligations
sections.title: Application of reporting obligations to professionals
sections.summary: Reporting obligations apply to accountants, advocates, notaries, and other legal professionals when handling client money, securities, or real estate. Trust and company service providers must also comply with reporting requirements when acting as formation agents, directors, or trustees. These obligations extend to specific financial services and professional activities.
sections.dtia_summary: Extends reporting obligations to professionals and service providers, which impacts data transfer assessments by requiring these entities to monitor and report transactions involving cross-border financial data flows.

requirements.text: Accountants, advocates, notaries, and other legal professionals must comply with reporting obligations when handling client money, securities, or real estate
Trust and company service providers must comply with reporting obligations when acting as formation agents, directors, or trustees

sections.source_quote: The obligations under this Part shall apply to accountants, advocates, notaries and other independent legal professionals who are sole practitioners, partners or employees within professional firms when preparing or carrying out transactions for their clients in the following situations

sections.heading: The Anti-Money Laundering Advisory Board
sections.title: Anti-Money Laundering Advisory Board composition
sections.summary: The Anti-Money Laundering Advisory Board consists of the Chairperson, Permanent Secretary, Attorney-General, Central Bank Governor, Police Commissioner, and representatives from financial institutions, the Institute of Certified Public Accountants, and the National Intelligence Service. The Board may co-opt additional members with expertise in anti-money laundering matters.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes a governance structure for anti-money laundering oversight, which impacts data transfer assessments by requiring coordination between regulatory bodies and financial institutions for cross-border compliance.

requirements.text: The Anti-Money Laundering Advisory Board consists of the Chairperson, Permanent Secretary, Attorney-General, Central Bank Governor, Police Commissioner, and representatives from financial institutions

sections.source_quote: There is established a Board to be known as the Anti-Money Laundering Advisory Board consisting of the Chairperson, who shall be appointed by the Cabinet Secretary from among members of the Board appointed under paragraphs (f) to (h)

sections.heading: Functions and powers of the Agency
sections.title: Agency functions and powers for asset recovery
sections.summary: The Agency implements Parts VII to XII of the Act and exercises all powers set forth therein. It has all necessary powers for its functions, receives funds from Parliament, manages recovered assets, maintains financial records, and prepares annual reports and estimates for the National Assembly.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the Agency's authority to implement asset recovery procedures, which may affect cross-border data transfers involving financial assets and recovered property.

requirements.text: The functions of the Agency shall be to implement the provisions of Parts VII to XII inclusive and to exercise all powers set forth therein
The Agency shall have all the powers necessary or expedient for the performance of its functions
The National Assembly shall allocate adequate funds to the Agency to enable the Agency perform its functions under this Act and any other written law and the budget shall be a separate vote
The funds of the Agency shall consist of monies provided for by Parliament for the purposes of the Agency
The funds of the Agency shall consist of not more than five percent of the total proceeds recovered or realized from any property seized or forfeited to the Government
The funds of the Agency shall consist of such monies or assets as may accrue to the Agency in the course of the exercise of its powers or the performance of its functions under this Act
The funds of the Agency shall consist of all monies from any other source provided, donated or granted to the Agency towards the achievement of the objects of the Agency

The Agency shall not accept any grant, gift, donation or bequests made on condition that the Agency performs any function or discharges any duty or obligation other than duties under the Constitution or this Act
The Agency shall disclose any grants, gifts, donations or bequests made to it in each financial year
The Agency Director shall be responsible for the financial statements of the Agency
The Agency shall prepare annual reports and financial statements for approval by the Advisory Board

sections.heading: Co-operation with the Agency
sections.title: Advisory Board structure and functions
sections.summary: This section establishes the Advisory Board, which includes the Cabinet Secretary, Attorney-General, Central Bank Governor, and other key officials. The Board advises the Agency on its powers, asset recovery policies, administration, budget, financial statements, and expenditures. It may co-opt members for committees and provide facilities for effective functioning.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the Advisory Board's role in overseeing asset recovery procedures, which may impact cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions and recovered assets.

requirements.text: The Advisory Board shall be responsible for advising and overseeing the Agency on the exercise of its powers and performance of its functions
The Advisory Board shall be responsible for advising and overseeing the Agency on asset recovery policies and strategic priorities of the Agency
The Advisory Board shall be responsible for advising and overseeing the Agency with respect to the administration of the Agency
The Advisory Board shall be responsible for approving the annual budget of the Agency
The Advisory Board shall be responsible for approving the annual reports and financial statements of the Agency
The Advisory Board shall be responsible for the expenditure of the Agency
The Advisory Board may establish such committees as may be necessary for the better carrying out of its functions
The Advisory Board may co-opt into any of its committees such other persons whose knowledge and skills are necessary for the better performance of its functions

The Agency shall provide the Advisory Board and any of its committees with such facilities and resources as may be required for the effective discharge of the functions of the Board or any of its committees

sections.source_quote: The Advisory Board shall be responsible for— (a) advising and overseeing the Agency on the exercise of its powers and performance of its functions; (b) advising and overseeing the Agency on asset recovery policies and strategic priorities of the Agency; (c) advising and overseeing the Agency with respect to the administration of the Agency; (d) approving the annual budget of the Agency; (e) approving the annual reports and financial statements of the Agency; and (f) the expenditure of the Agency. The Advisory Board may establish such committees as may be necessary for the better carrying out of its functions under this Act. The Agency shall provide the Advisory Board and any of its committees with such facilities and resources as may be required for the effective discharge of the functions of the Board or any of its committees.

sections.heading: Application for forfeiture order
sections.title: Application for forfeiture of property
sections.summary: The Agency Director may apply to the High Court for forfeiture of property subject to preservation orders, provided they have served notice to affected persons. This requires fourteen-day notice to those who served notice under section 83(3), and allows applicants to oppose the order or seek exclusions from the forfeiture.
sections.dtia_summary: Application for forfeiture of property affects data transfers by requiring notice to affected parties before forfeiting assets, ensuring transparency in property-related data handling.

sections.source_quote: 90. Application for forfeiture order (1) If a preservation order is in force, the Agency Director may apply to the High Court for an order forfeiting to the Government all or any of the property that is subject to the preservation order.

sections.heading: Monitoring orders
sections.title: Monitoring orders for financial transactions
sections.summary: This section authorizes authorized officers to request monitoring orders from courts to compel financial institutions to disclose transaction information for suspected serious offences. Monitoring orders require specific details about accounts and transactions, and violations may result in fines or imprisonment for financial institutions or individuals.

sections.title: Regulations for Reporting and Due Diligence
sections.summary: The Cabinet Secretary must make regulations covering information reporting, client designation, legal costs, high-risk customers, and other matters. Regulations must require reporting institutions to verify customer identities, maintain transaction records, report suspicious activities, and implement internal anti-money laundering compliance programs. These rules apply to financial institutions handling money laundering risks.

Regulations must designate persons for money laundering purposes

Regulations must mandate transaction record maintenance
Regulations must require suspicious activity reporting
Regulations must establish internal reporting procedures for money laundering

Regulations (1) The Cabinet Secretary shall make Regulations— (a) with regard to the nature of the information contemplated in section 44 and the manner in which it is to be reported; (b) with regard to the designation of persons for purposes of section 47; (c) prescribing maximum allowable costs for legal services in connection with an application for a preservation of property order or forfeiture order; (d) providing for high risk customers or clients; (e) providing for any matter which he may consider necessary or expedient to prescribe or to regulate in order to achieve the objects of this Act.

sections.title: Majority Decision Rule
sections.summary: Decisions on matters before the Board or Advisory Board require a majority vote of present members, with the chairperson having a casting vote in case of a tie.
sections.dtia_summary: Majority decision rules ensure consistent and transparent governance, which indirectly affects data handling compliance through structured decision-making.

requirements.text: Decisions on matters require majority vote of present members
Chairperson has casting vote in case of equality of votes

sections.source_quote: 4. Decision of the Board or Advisory Board to be by majority Unless a unanimous decision is reached, a decision on any matter before the Committee shall be by a majority of votes of the members present and in the case of an equality of votes, the Chairperson or the member presiding shall have a casting vote.

sections.heading: Defence
sections.title: Board members must disclose conflicts of interest
sections.summary: Board or Advisory Board members must disclose any direct or indirect interest in matters being considered by the Board immediately upon becoming aware of the facts. Disclosed members are prohibited from participating in deliberations on the matter to avoid conflicts of interest.

requirements.text: A Board or Advisory Board member who has a direct or indirect interest in a matter being considered must disclose the nature of their interest to the Board as soon as possible
A Board or Advisory Board member who has a direct or indirect interest in a matter must not be present during deliberations on that matter

sections.source_quote: 6. Board or Advisory Board member to disclose interest A member of the Board or Advisory Board who has a direct or indirect interest in a matter being considered or to be considered by the Board or Advisory Board shall, as soon as possible after the relevant facts concerning the matter have come to his knowledge, disclose the nature of his interest to the Board or Advisory Board and shall not be present during any deliberations on the matter.

sections.heading: Financial promotion of an offence
sections.title: Board must record and maintain meeting minutes
sections.summary: The Board or Advisory Board must record and maintain minutes of all meetings, with each set of minutes confirmed at the next meeting and signed by the presiding member. This ensures accountability and transparency in decision-making processes.

The Board or Advisory Board to cause minutes to be recorded and kept The Board or Advisory Board shall cause the minutes of all proceedings of its meetings to be recorded and kept, and the minutes of each meeting shall be confirmed by the Board or Advisory Board at the next meeting of the Board or Advisory Board and signed by the Chairperson or the member presiding at the meeting.

sections.heading: Tipping off
sections.title: Quorum requirements for Board meetings
sections.summary: A quorum for Board or Advisory Board meetings requires at least six members present. If a quorum is absent due to a member's exclusion under conflict of interest rules, the remaining members may either postpone the matter or proceed without quorum if they deem it expedient.

requirements.text: Six members shall constitute a quorum for the conduct of business at any meeting of the Board or Advisory Board
When there is no quorum due to exclusion of a member under paragraph 6, the other members may postpone consideration of the matter or proceed as if there was quorum

sections.source_quote: 8. Quorum (1) Subject to subsection (2), six members shall constitute a quorum for the conduct of business at any meeting of the Board or Advisory Board. (2) When there is no quorum at or for the continuation of a meeting of the Board or Advisory Board only because of the exclusion of a member under paragraph 6, the other members present may, if they deem it expedient so to do— (a) postpone the consideration of that matter until there is a quorum; or (b) proceed to consider and decide the matter as if there was quorum.

sections.heading: Misrepresentation
sections.title: Misrepresentation in reporting to financial institutions
sections.summary: A person commits an offence if they knowingly make false statements or provide false documents to reporting institutions, supervisory bodies, or the Centre. This section prohibits intentional deception in financial reporting to authorities. The offence applies to individuals and entities involved in financial transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires accurate reporting of financial transactions to prevent false declarations that could facilitate illicit financial flows across borders.

requirements.text: Knowingly making false, fictitious or fraudulent statements to reporting institutions
Knowingly providing false documents containing false statements

sections.source_quote: A person who knowingly makes a false, fictitious or fraudulent statement or representation, or makes, or provides, any false document, knowing the same to contain any false, fictitious or fraudulent statement or entry, to a reporting institution, or to a supervisory body or to the Centre, commits an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial Data, AML, cross-border transfer relevance

sections.heading: Failure to comply with the provisions of this Act
sections.title: Failure to comply with reporting obligations
sections.summary: Reporting institutions that fail to comply with sections 44, 45, 46, or regulations commit an offence. Courts assess compliance considering industry practices and relevant guidance from supervisory authorities. This section establishes accountability for non-compliance with financial reporting requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to maintain compliance with reporting obligations to prevent illicit financial activities across borders.

requirements.text: Reporting institutions must comply with sections 44, 45, 46, or regulations

sections.source_quote: A reporting institution that fails to comply with any of the requirements of sections 44, 45 and 46, or of any regulations, commits an offence.

sections.heading: Conveyance of monetary instruments to or from Kenya
sections.title: Reporting of monetary instruments crossing Kenya
sections.summary: Persons must report monetary instruments exceeding thresholds to authorized entities before crossing Kenya. Authorized officers must send copies to the Centre and may temporarily seize instruments suspected of being tainted. Seized instruments require detailed documentation and retention for seven years. Failure to report or misrepresentation constitutes an offence.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates reporting of cross-border monetary transfers to prevent illicit financial flows and requires detailed documentation for seizure and retention of financial instruments.

requirements.text: Report monetary instruments exceeding thresholds to authorized entities before crossing Kenya
Authorized officers must send copies of reports to the Centre
Seized monetary instruments must be documented with officer details, seizure time, location, and instrument descriptions
Seized instruments must be accompanied by a formal notice of intent to initiate forfeiture proceedings
Authorized officers must inquire about origin and intended use of monetary instruments
Information on declared monetary instruments must be retained for at least seven years

sections.source_quote: A person intending to convey monetary instruments in excess of the amount prescribed in the Second Schedule to or from Kenya shall, before so doing, report the particulars concerning that conveyance to a person authorised by the regulations for that purpose.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border financial data reporting and seizure requirements

sections.heading: Misuse of information
sections.title: Misuse of information in investigations
sections.summary: A person commits an offence if they knowingly alert others who could prejudice an investigation into financial misconduct. Information collected under this section must comply with the Data Protection Act, 2019. This section addresses misuse of sensitive financial information during investigations.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibits sharing information that could disrupt financial investigations and requires compliance with data protection standards for sensitive financial data.

requirements.text: Knowingly alerting others who could prejudice an investigation into financial misconduct

sections.source_quote: A person who knows or ought reasonably to have known—(a) that information has been disclosed under the provisions of Part II; or (b) that an investigation is being, or may be, conducted as a result of that disclosure, and directly or indirectly alerts, or brings information to the attention of another person who will or is likely to prejudice the investigation, commits an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: Financial data misuse in investigations

sections.heading: Hindering a person in performance of functions under this Act
sections.title: Hindering enforcement actions
sections.summary: A person who obstructs a receiver, police officer, or other person performing duties under this Act commits an offence. This section addresses interference with financial investigations and enforcement actions.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibits actions that hinder financial investigations, ensuring smooth enforcement of cross-border financial regulations.

requirements.text: Hindering a receiver, police officer, or other person in the exercise of powers under this Act

sections.source_quote: A person who hinders a receiver, a police officer or any other person in the exercise, performance or carrying out of their powers, functions or duties under this Act, commits an offence.
sections.relevance_reason: AML enforcement obstruction

sections.heading: Penalties
sections.title: Penalties for financial offences
sections.summary: Violations of sections 3, 4, 7, 5, 8, 11(1), 13, or 9, 10, 14 incur fines and imprisonment. Penalties vary by offence type and severity, with fines up to 25 million shillings for corporations and up to 5 million shillings for individuals. The penalties apply to both individuals and entities.
sections.dtia_summary: Imposes strict penalties for non-compliance with financial reporting and anti-money laundering regulations, emphasizing accountability for cross-border financial misconduct.

requirements.text: Violations of specified sections incur fines and imprisonment

sections.source_quote: A person who contravenes any of the provisions of sections 3, 4 or 7 is on conviction liable—(a) in the case of a natural person, to imprisonment for a term not exceeding fourteen years, or a fine not exceeding five million shillings or the amount of the value of the property involved in the offence, whichever is the higher, or to both the fine and imprisonment;
sections.relevance_reason: Penalties for financial offences under AML

sections.heading: Secrecy obligations overridden
sections.title: Secrecy obligations overridden by anti-money laundering acts
sections.summary: This section states that provisions of the Proceeds of Crime and Anti-Money Laundering Act override any secrecy or disclosure restrictions from other laws. It also clarifies that no liability arises from disclosing information in compliance with this Act's obligations, whether under law, common law, or agreements.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that financial institutions' disclosures under the Act are not subject to secrecy obligations, which is critical for DTIAs involving cross-border data sharing in anti-money laundering contexts.
sections.authority: Proceeds of Crime and Anti-Money Laundering Act

requirements.text: Provisions of this Act override any secrecy or disclosure restrictions from other laws
No liability arises from disclosing information in compliance with this Act's obligations

sections.source_quote: The provisions of this Act shall override any obligation as to secrecy or other restriction on disclosure of information imposed by any other law or otherwise. No liability based on a breach of an obligation as to secrecy or any restriction on the disclosure of information, whether imposed by any law, the common law or any agreement, shall arise from a disclosure of any information in compliance with any obligation imposed by this Act
sections.relevance_reason: AML override secrecy for financial data disclosure

sections.heading: Client advocate relationship

sections.summary: This section preserves the client-advocate privilege for communications related to legal advice and proceedings, but allows the High Court to order disclosure of information in specific anti-money laundering investigations without breaching this privilege.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines when client-advocate privilege applies in financial investigations, which impacts DTIAs for cross-border data sharing involving legal professionals.

sections.source_quote: Notwithstanding the provisions of section 17, nothing in this Act shall affect or be deemed to affect the relationship between an advocate and his client with regard to communication of privileged information between the advocate and the client.

sections.heading: Immunity where actions are exercised in good faith

sections.summary: This section provides immunity from legal action for reporting institutions, government entities, and their officers who act with due diligence and in good faith when performing functions under the Act.

requirements.text: No legal action against reporting institutions or officers acting with due diligence and in good faith under this Act

sections.heading: Protection of information and informers
sections.title: Confidentiality of information and informers
sections.summary: This section mandates confidentiality of information received by the Financial Reporting Centre and the identity of informers, with exceptions for specific legal purposes, witness protection, and court-ordered disclosures.

sections.heading: Establishment of a Financial Reporting Centre
sections.title: Establishment of Financial Reporting Centre
sections.summary: This section establishes the Financial Reporting Centre as a body corporate with perpetual succession, capable of suing and being sued, holding property, entering contracts, and performing necessary functions under the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the legal structure of the Financial Reporting Centre, which is critical for DTIAs involving cross-border data transfers and financial investigations.

requirements.text: Financial Reporting Centre is established as a body corporate with perpetual succession

sections.source_quote: There is established a centre to be known as the Financial Reporting Centre, (hereinafter referred to as the "Centre") which shall be a body corporate, with perpetual succession and a common seal and shall be capable, in its corporate name, of— (a) suing and being sued; (b) taking, purchasing or otherwise acquiring, holding or disposing of movable and immovable property; (c) entering into contracts; (d) doing or performing such other things or acts necessary for the proper performance of its functions under this Act which may lawfully be done by a body corporate.

sections.heading: Objectives of the Centre
sections.title: Centre's objectives for anti-money laundering
sections.summary: This section outlines the Centre's objectives to identify crime proceeds, combat money laundering and terrorism financing, share information with relevant authorities, and exchange data internationally while ensuring compliance with international standards.

sections.heading: Definitions applicable to Parts VI-XII
sections.title: Definitions for asset recovery procedures
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in Parts VI to XII, including how property ownership, property transfers, and proceeds of crime are interpreted in legal contexts. It clarifies that a person who has benefited from an offence includes those who received or retained proceeds of crime at any time before or after the Act's commencement.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify how property and proceeds of crime are treated in asset recovery, which may affect cross-border data transfers involving financial assets.

requirements.text: A reference to a person who holds property shall be construed as a reference to a person who has any interest in the property
If the estate of that person has been sequestrated, also to the trustees of his estate
If the person is a company or other legal entity which is being wound up, also to the liquidator thereof
A reference to a person who transfers property to another person shall be construed as a reference to a person who transfers or grants to any other person any interest in the property
Anything received in connection with an offence shall be construed as a reference also to anything received as a result of and in connection with the commission of that offence
For the purposes of Parts VI to XII, a person will have benefited from an offence if that person has at any time received or retained any proceeds of crime

sections.source_quote: (1) In Parts VI to XII, except where it is inconsistent with the context or clearly inappropriate, any reference to— (a) a person who holds property shall be construed as a reference to a person who has any interest in the property, and— (i) if the estate of that person has been sequestrated, also to the trustees of his estate; or (ii) if the person is a company or other legal entity which is being wound up, also to the liquidator thereof; (b) a person who transfers property to another person shall be construed as a reference to a person who transfers or grants to any other person any interest in the property; (c) anything received in connection with an offence shall be construed as a reference also to anything received as a result of and in connection with the commission of that offence. (3) For the purposes of Parts VI to XII, a person will have benefited from an offence if that person has at any time, whether before or after the commencement of this Act, received or retained any proceeds of crime.

requirements.text: Establish a body to be known as Assets Recovery Agency, which shall be a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal

The Agency shall be capable of holding and alienating movable and immovable property
The Agency shall be capable of borrowing and lending money
The Agency shall be capable of doing and performing all such other acts or things as may be lawfully done by a body corporate
The Attorney-General shall appoint a fit, competent and proper person to be the Agency Director
The Agency Director shall hold office for a term of four years and be eligible for re-appointment for one further term of four years
To be appointed as Agency Director, a person must hold a degree in law, finance, accounting or any other relevant degree from a university recognised in Kenya
To be appointed as Agency Director, a person must have at least fifteen years work experience, of which at least five shall be in senior management
The Agency Director may, with the approval of the Attorney-General, obtain staff on secondment and on terms and conditions of service
Anything which the Agency Director is authorized or required to do may be done by a member of staff of the Agency or a person providing services under arrangements made by the Agency Director

sections.source_quote: There is established a body to be known as Assets Recovery Agency, which shall be a body corporate with perpetual succession and a common seal, and shall in its corporate name, be capable of— (a) suing and being sued; (b) holding and alienating movable and immovable property; (c) borrowing and lending money; (d) doing and performing all such other acts or things as may be lawfully done by a body corporate. The Attorney-General shall upon recommendation by the Advisory Board appoint a fit, competent and proper person to be the Director of the Agency (hereinafter referred to as the "Agency Director"). The Agency Director shall hold office for a term of four years and shall be eligible for re-appointment for one further term of four years. For a person to be appointed as the Agency Director, that person shall— (a) hold a degree in law, finance, accounting or any other relevant degree from a university recognised in Kenya; (b) have at least fifteen years work experience, of which at least five shall be in senior management; (c) meet such other requirements that may be specified by the Advisory Board.

sections.heading: Functions and powers of the Agency
sections.title: Agency functions and powers for asset recovery
sections.summary: The Agency implements Parts VII to XII of the Act and exercises all powers set forth therein. It has all necessary powers for its functions, receives funds from Parliament, manages recovered assets, maintains financial records, and prepares annual reports and estimates for the National Assembly.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the Agency's authority to implement asset recovery procedures, which may affect cross-border data transfers involving financial assets and recovered property.

requirements.text: The functions of the Agency shall be to implement the provisions of Parts VII to XII inclusive and to exercise all powers set forth therein
The Agency shall have all the powers necessary or expedient for the performance of its functions
The National Assembly shall allocate adequate funds to the Agency to enable the Agency perform its functions under this Act and any other written law and the budget shall be a separate vote
The funds of the Agency shall consist of monies provided for by Parliament for the purposes of the Agency
The funds of the Agency shall consist of not more than five percent of the total proceeds recovered or realized from any property seized or forfeited to the Government
The funds of the Agency shall consist of such monies or assets as may accrue to the Agency in the course of the exercise of its powers or the performance of its functions under this Act
The funds of the Agency shall consist of all monies from any other source provided, donated or granted to the Agency towards the achievement of the objects of the Agency

The Agency shall not accept any grant, gift, donation or bequests made on condition that the Agency performs any function or discharges any duty or obligation other than duties under the Constitution or this Act
The Agency shall disclose any grants, gifts, donations or bequests made to it in each financial year
The Agency Director shall be responsible for the financial statements of the Agency
The Agency shall prepare annual reports and financial statements for approval by the Advisory Board

It may co-opt members for committees and provide facilities for effective functioning.

The Advisory Board shall be responsible for advising and overseeing the Agency on asset recovery policies and strategic priorities of the Agency
The Advisory Board shall be responsible for advising and overseeing the Agency with respect to the administration of the Agency
The Advisory Board shall be responsible for approving the annual budget of the Agency
The Advisory Board shall be responsible for approving the annual reports and financial statements of the Agency
The Advisory Board shall be responsible for the expenditure of the Agency

sections.source_quote: The Advisory Board shall be responsible for— (a) advising and overseeing the Agency on the exercise of its powers and performance of its functions; (b) advising and overseeing the Agency on asset recovery policies and strategic priorities of the Agency; (c) advising and overseeing the Agency with respect to the administration of the Agency; (d) approving the annual budget of the Agency; (e) approving the annual reports and financial statements of the Agency; and (f) the expenditure of the Agency. sections.heading: Nature of proceedings
sections.title: Nature of proceedings for confiscation orders
sections.summary: Proceedings for confiscation orders or restraint orders are classified as civil in this Act. Civil evidence rules apply to these proceedings. This section defines the legal nature of the proceedings to ensure consistent application of civil procedural standards.

requirements.text: Proceedings on application for a confiscation order or restraint order are civil
Rules of evidence applicable in civil proceedings apply to proceedings on application for a confiscation order or restraint order

sections.source_quote: 56. Nature of proceedings (1) For the purposes of this Part, proceedings on application for a confiscation order or restraint order are civil. (2) The rules of evidence applicable in civil proceedings shall apply to proceedings on application for a confiscation order or a restraint order.

sections.heading: Realizable property
sections.title: Realizable property in confiscation proceedings
sections.summary: Realizable property includes property held by the defendant or property held by someone who received a gift from the defendant. Property is not realizable if a forfeiture order is in force. This section specifies what constitutes realizable property for confiscation purposes.

requirements.text: Realizable property includes property held by the defendant
Realizable property includes property held by a person who received a gift from the defendant
Property is not realizable if a forfeiture order is in force

sections.source_quote: 57. Realizable property (1) Subject to the provisions of subsection (2), the following property shall be realizable in terms of this Part— (a) any property held by the defendant concerned; and (b) any property held by a person to whom that defendant has directly or indirectly made any affected gift. (2) Property shall not be realizable property so long as a forfeiture order is in force in respect thereof.
sections.relevance_reason: Purely financial/legal confiscation, no data handling

sections.heading: Value of property
sections.title: Value of property for confiscation orders
sections.summary: The value of property for confiscation purposes is determined by market value minus encumbrances. If the recipient holds property, the value is the market value at the time of receipt. This section provides specific valuation rules for property in confiscation proceedings.

requirements.text: Value of property, other than money, is market value less encumbrances if another person holds an interest
Value of property, other than money, is market value if no other person holds an interest
Value of payment or reward is the value at the time of receipt unless subsection (3) applies
If the recipient holds property, the value is the market value at the time of receipt
If the recipient holds property representing received property, the value is the market value at the time of receipt

sections.source_quote: 58. Value of property (1) For the purposes of this Part the value of property, other than money, in relation to any person holding the property, shall be— (a) where any other person holds an interest in the property, the market value of the property, less the amount required to discharge any encumbrance on the property; and (b) where no other person holds an interest in the property, the market value of the property. (2) Notwithstanding the provisions of subsection (1), any reference in this Part to the value at a particular time of a payment or reward, shall be construed as a reference to— (a) the value of the payment or reward at the time when the recipient received it; or (b) where subsection (3) applies, the value set out in that subsection, whichever is the higher. (3) If, at the particular time referred to in subsection (2) the recipient holds— (a) the property, other than cash, which that person received, the applicable value shall be the value of the property at the particular time; or (b) property which directly or indirectly represents in his hands the property which he received, the applicable value shall be the value of the property, in so far as it represents the property which he received, at the relevant time.
sections.relevance_reason: Purely financial/confiscation rules, no data handling

sections.heading: Gifts
sections.title: Gifts in confiscation proceedings
sections.summary: A defendant is deemed to have made a gift if they transferred property for significantly less consideration than its value. The gift consists of the difference between the property's value and the consideration received. This section defines what constitutes a gift for confiscation purposes.

requirements.text: A defendant is deemed to have made a gift if they transferred property for significantly less consideration than its value
The gift consists of the difference between the property's value and the consideration received

sections.source_quote: 59. Gifts (1) For the purposes of this Part, a defendant shall be deemed to have made a gift if he has transferred any property to any other person directly or indirectly for a consideration which is significantly less than the value of the property. (2) For the purposes of subsection (2) the gift which a defendant is deemed to have made shall consist of that share in the property transferred by the defendant which is equal to the difference between the value of that property as a whole and the consideration received by the defendant in return.

sections.heading: Conclusion of proceedings against defendant
sections.title: Conclusion of proceedings against defendant
sections.summary: Proceedings against a defendant are concluded when they are acquitted, sentenced without a confiscation order, have their conviction set aside on appeal, or satisfy a confiscation order. This section defines when proceedings are finalized for confiscation purposes.

requirements.text: Proceedings against a defendant are concluded when acquitted or found not guilty
Proceedings against a defendant are concluded when sentenced without a confiscation order
Proceedings against a defendant are concluded when conviction is set aside on review or appeal
Proceedings against a defendant are concluded when the defendant satisfies a confiscation order

sections.source_quote: 60. Conclusion of proceedings against defendant For the purposes of this Part, proceedings against a defendant shall be concluded when— (a) the defendant is acquitted or found not guilty of an offence; (b) subject to section 61(2), the court convicting the defendant of an offence, sentences the defendant without making a confiscation order against him; (c) the conviction in respect of an offence is set aside on review or appeal; or (d) the defendant satisfies the confiscation order made against him.
sections.relevance_reason: Defines when proceedings conclude for confiscation purposes

sections.heading: Confiscation orders
sections.title: Confiscation orders for convicted defendants
sections.summary: Courts may order defendants to pay the government for benefits derived from offences, including related criminal activities. The amount cannot exceed the value of proceeds or related activities. Courts may conduct inquiries later if needed. This section outlines confiscation order procedures and limits.

requirements.text: Courts may inquire into benefits derived from offences, related offences, or related criminal activities
Courts may make orders for payment to the government of amounts they consider appropriate
The amount ordered cannot exceed the value of proceeds or related activities
The amount ordered cannot exceed what might be realized
Courts may indicate they will hold an inquiry later if it would unreasonably delay sentencing
Courts may allow payment in a specified period if the defendant needs time

sections.source_quote: 61. Confiscation orders (1) Whenever a defendant is convicted of an offence, the court convicting the defendant shall, on the application of the Attorney-General, the Agency Director or of its own motion, inquire into any benefit which the defendant may have derived from— (a) that offence; (b) any other offence of which the defendant has been convicted at the same trial; and (c) any criminal activity which the court finds to be sufficiently related to that offence, and, if the court finds that the defendant has so benefited, the court shall, in addition to any punishment which it may impose, make an order against the defendant for the payment to the Government of any amount it considers appropriate and the court may make any further orders as it may deem fit to ensure the effectiveness and fairness of that order. (2) The amount which a court may order the defendant to pay to the Government under subsection (1)— (a) shall not exceed the value of the defendant's proceeds of the offences or related criminal activities referred to in that subsection, as determined by the court in accordance with the provisions of this Part; or (b) if the court is satisfied that the amount which is just as contemplated in section 63(1) is less than the value referred to in paragraph (a), the amount payable shall not exceed an amount which, in the opinion of the court might be so realized. (3) A court convicting a defendant may, when passing sentence, indicate that it will hold an inquiry as contemplated in subsection (1) at a later stage if— (a) it is satisfied that inquiry would unreasonably delay the sentencing of the defendant; or (b) the Attorney-General applies to the court to first sentence the defendant and the court is satisfied that it is reasonable and justifiable to do so in the circumstances. (4) If the judge or magistrate who convicted the defendant is absent or for any other reason not available, any judge or magistrate of the same court shall consider an application referred to in subsection (1) and hold the inquiry referred to in that subsection and that person may, in the proceedings, take such steps as the judge or magistrate who is absent or not available could lawfully have taken. (5) A court before which proceedings under this section are pending, may, in considering an application under subsection (1)— (i) refer to the evidence and proceedings at the trial; (ii) hear further oral evidence or take documentary evidence as the court may deem fit; (iii) direct the Agency Director to tender to the court the affidavit referred to in section 64(1); and (iv) direct a defendant to tender to the court an affidavit referred to under section  64(5). (6) The amount ordered to be paid under a confiscation order shall be paid on the making of the order: Provided that if the defendant indicates to the court that he needs time to pay the amount ordered to be paid, the court making the confiscation order may make an order allowing payment to be made in a specified period.
sections.relevance_reason: No data processing or privacy elements

sections.heading: Value of proceeds of crime
sections.title: Determining value of crime proceeds excluding certain assets
sections.summary: This section specifies how courts calculate the value of a defendant's proceeds of crime by summing property, services, advantages, or benefits received after the Act's commencement. It excludes property that was part of a forfeiture order or confiscated assets, ensuring only relevant proceeds are considered in sentencing.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines how to calculate crime proceeds value for cross-border asset forfeiture, relevant for DTIAs involving international asset tracing.

requirements.text: The value of the defendant's proceeds of crime shall be the sum of the value of property, services, advantages, benefits or rewards received, retained or derived after the Act's commencement.
Courts shall exclude property that was part of a forfeiture order or confiscated assets when determining proceeds value.

sections.source_quote: 62. Value of proceeds of crime (1) Subject to subsection (2), the value of the defendant's proceeds of crime shall be the sum of the value of the property, services, advantages, benefits or rewards received, retained or derived by him at any time after the commencement of this Act in connection with the offence committed by him or any other person.

sections.heading: Amount which might be realized
sections.title: Calculating realizable value for confiscation orders
sections.summary: This section details how courts determine the realizable value of assets for confiscation orders, including adjustments for inflation, court-recognized obligations, and specific valuation methods for property and gifts. It ensures accurate asset valuation before confiscation.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides valuation methodology for cross-border asset confiscation, critical for DTIAs involving international asset recovery.

requirements.text: The amount which might be realized is the sum of realizable property and affected gifts minus obligations with priority recognized by the court.
The value of an affected gift is adjusted for inflation or determined by the court as just.
Courts must consider the value of property at the time it was received or its representative value.
Obligations with priority include court-ordered fines, debts, or insolvency claims.
Courts must afford all property holders an opportunity to make representations before determining realizable amounts.

sections.source_quote: 63. Amount which might be realized (1) For the purposes of sections 61(2)(b) and 67(4)(a), the amount which might be realized at the time of the making of a confiscation order against a defendant shall be the amount equal to the sum of the values at that time of all— (a) realizable property held by the defendant; and (b) affected gifts made by the defendant, less the sum of all obligations, if any, of the defendant having priority and which the court may recognize for this purpose.

sections.heading: Statements relating to proceeds of crime
sections.title: Affidavit procedures for crime proceeds evidence
sections.summary: This section outlines the process for submitting affidavits related to crime proceeds, including serving copies to defendants, allowing disputes on allegations, and treating unchallenged allegations as conclusive evidence. It ensures transparency in evidence handling.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines affidavit procedures for cross-border asset evidence, relevant for DTIAs involving international evidence collection.

requirements.text: The Agency Director may tender an affidavit by the defendant or another person to the court for crime proceeds inquiries.
Copies of affidavits must be served on the defendant.
Defendants may dispute affidavit allegations and state grounds for disputes.
Unchallenged affidavit allegations are considered conclusive evidence.
The Agency Director may admit affidavit allegations as conclusive evidence.

sections.source_quote: 64. Statements relating to proceeds of crime (1) The Agency Director may or, if so directed by the court, shall tender to the court an affidavit by the defendant or any other person in connection with any matter which is being inquired into by the court under section 61(1), or which relates to the determination of the value of a defendant's proceeds of crime.

sections.heading: Evidence relating to proceeds of crime
sections.title: Prima facie evidence for crime proceeds valuation
sections.summary: This section establishes prima facie evidence rules for determining crime proceeds, including property derived from offenses, benefits from crimes, and expenditures linked to criminal activities. It simplifies court assessments of asset connections.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides prima facie evidence standards for cross-border asset tracing, relevant for DTIAs involving international crime proceeds.

requirements.text: If a defendant lacks legitimate income sources, the court accepts property as prima facie evidence of crime proceeds.
Failure to disclose facts or providing false information constitutes prima facie evidence of crime proceeds.
Property received after conviction is prima facie evidence of crime proceeds.
Expenditures incurred post-conviction are prima facie evidence of being funded by crime proceeds.
Property received without interest is prima facie evidence of crime proceeds.

sections.source_quote: 65. Evidence relating to proceeds of crime (1) For the purpose of determining whether a defendant has derived a benefit in an inquiry under section 61(1), if it is found that the defendant did not, at the fixed date, have legitimate sources of income sufficient to justify the interests in any property that he holds, the court shall accept this fact as prima facie evidence that the interests form part of the benefit.

sections.heading: Procedure where person absconds or dies
sections.title: Inquiries for absconding or deceased defendants
sections.summary: This section governs procedures for courts to inquire into benefits derived from crimes when defendants abscond or die, including appointing receivers, realizing assets, and ensuring representations from estate administrators. It addresses post-conviction asset recovery.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines procedures for cross-border asset recovery when defendants are absent or deceased, relevant for DTIAs involving international asset forfeiture.

requirements.text: Courts may inquire into benefits derived from crimes if defendants abscond or die without conviction.
The estate administrator may appear before the court to make representations.
Courts may appoint receivers for realizable property.
Courts may authorize asset realization under Part VIII.
Courts must afford property holders an opportunity to make representations before orders.

sections.source_quote: 67. Procedure where person absconds or dies (1) If a court is satisfied that— (a) a person had been charged with an offence; or (b) a person had been convicted of any offence; or (c) a warrant for that person's arrest had been issued and that the attendance of that person in court could not be secured after all reasonable steps were taken to execute that warrant; and (d) there are reasonable grounds to believe that a confiscation order would have been made against that person were it not for his continued absence, the court may, on an application by the Agency Director, inquire into any benefit he may have derived from that offence.

sections.heading: Restraint orders
sections.title: Restraint orders for property control
sections.summary: This section details restraint orders that prohibit individuals from dealing with property, including provisions for living expenses, legal costs, and business continuity. It ensures property control during criminal investigations.
sections.dtia_summary: Provides mechanisms for cross-border property control, relevant for DTIAs involving international asset freezes.

requirements.text: The Agency Director may apply ex parte for restraint orders prohibiting property dealings.
Restraint orders cover realizable property and property that would become realizable if transferred.
Courts may issue temporary restraint orders if criminal investigations are ongoing or criminal lifestyles are suspected.
Restraint orders must provide notice periods for affected persons.
Restraint orders may cover living expenses, legal costs, and business continuity for the restrained person.
Courts may order the seizure of movable property under restraint orders.
Courts may vary or rescind restraint orders if they cause undue hardship.

sections.source_quote: 68. Restraint orders (1) The Agency Director may apply to a court ex parte for an order prohibiting any person, subject to such conditions and exceptions as may be specified in the order, from dealing in any manner with any property to which the order relates.

sections.heading: Cases in which restraint order may be made
sections.title: Conditions for court to issue restraint order
sections.summary: A court may issue a restraint order if a prosecution has been instituted against the defendant, a confiscation order has been made or there are reasonable grounds for believing a confiscation order may be made, and the proceedings have not concluded. Alternatively, the court may issue a restraint order if it is satisfied a person will be charged with an offence and there are reasonable grounds for believing a confiscation order may be made. The order remains in effect until the person is charged.
sections.dtia_summary: Restriction on when a court can issue a restraint order for property seizure during confiscation proceedings; relevant for cross-border asset freezes.

requirements.text: A court may exercise powers under section 68(1) when a prosecution has been instituted against the defendant
A court may exercise powers under section 68(1) when a confiscation order has been made against the defendant
A court may exercise powers under section 68(1) when there are reasonable grounds for believing a confiscation order may be made against the defendant
A court may exercise powers under section 68(1) when the proceedings against the defendant have not been concluded
A court may exercise powers under section 68(1) when it is satisfied a person is to be charged with an offence
A court may exercise powers under section 68(1) when there are reasonable grounds for believing a confiscation order may be made against that person
Where a court has made a restraint order under subsection (1)(b), it shall rescind the order if the relevant person is not charged within a reasonable period

sections.source_quote: 69. Cases in which restraint order may be made (1) A court may exercise the powers conferred on it by section 68(1)— (a) when— (i) a prosecution for an offence has been instituted against the defendant concerned; and (ii) either a confiscation order has been made against that defendant or it appears to the court that there are reasonable grounds for believing that a confiscation order may be made against that defendant; and (iii) the proceedings against that defendant have not been concluded; or (b) when— (i) that court is satisfied that a person is to be charged with an offence; and (ii) it appears to the court that there are reasonable grounds for believing that a confiscation order may be made against that person.

sections.heading: Order to remain in force pending appeal
sections.title: Continuation of orders during appeal
sections.summary: A restraint order and any related property seizure orders remain in effect during an appeal against a confiscation decision. This ensures that assets are not released until the appeal outcome is known.
sections.dtia_summary: Ensures asset freezes continue during appeals; critical for maintaining financial integrity across jurisdictions.

requirements.text: A restraint order and an order authorising the seizure of property shall remain in force pending the outcome of any appeal against the decision concerned

sections.source_quote: 70. Order to remain in force pending appeal A restraint order and an order authorising the seizure of the property concerned or other ancillary order which is in force at the time of any decision by the court in relation to the making of a confiscation order, shall remain in force pending the outcome of any appeal against the decision concerned.

sections.heading: Seizure of property subject to restraint order
sections.title: Police seizure of property under restraint order
sections.summary: Police may seize realizable property to prevent its disposal or removal if they have reasonable grounds to believe it will be done. Seized property must be handled according to court directions.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for immediate seizure of assets to prevent cross-border transfers during legal proceedings.

requirements.text: A police officer may seize property if they have reasonable grounds to believe it will be disposed of or removed contrary to a restraint order
Property seized under subsection (1) shall be dealt with in accordance with the directions of the court that made the relevant restraint order

sections.source_quote: 71. Seizure of property subject to restraint order (1) In order to prevent any realizable property from being disposed of or removed contrary to a restraint order, a police officer may seize that property if he has reasonable grounds to believe that the property will be so disposed of or removed. (2) Property seized under subsection (1) shall be dealt with in accordance with the directions of the court that made the relevant restraint order.

sections.heading: Appointment of manager in respect of property subject to restraint order
sections.title: Appointment of property manager under restraint order
sections.summary: Courts may appoint a manager to handle property for a person under a restraint order. The manager can perform acts like taking care of property, administering assets, or selling perishable items. The person affected may apply to vary or rescind the order or discharge the manager.
sections.dtia_summary: Manages asset custody during legal proceedings; critical for cross-border financial asset preservation.

requirements.text: A court may appoint a manager to perform acts on behalf of a person against whom the restraint order has been made
A court may appoint a manager to take care of the property
A court may appoint a manager to administer the property
A court may appoint a manager to carry on a business or undertaking with due regard to applicable law
A court may appoint a manager to sell or dispose of perishable property
A court may order the person to surrender property to the manager within a determined period
A person affected by an order under subsection (1)(b) may apply for variation of rescission of the order
A person affected by an order under subsection (1)(b) may apply for variation of the terms of the appointment of the manager or discharge of that manager
The court may at any time vary or rescind the order
The court may at any time vary the terms of the appointment of the manager or discharge that manager
The court shall discharge the manager concerned if the relevant restraint order is rescinded
The court may make an order relating to the fees and expenditure of the manager
The court may order payment of the manager's fees from confiscated proceeds if a confiscation order is made

sections.source_quote: 72. Appointment of manager in respect of property subject to restraint order (1) Where a court has made a restraint order, that court may, at any time— (a) appoint a manager to do any one or more of the following on behalf of a person against whom the restraint order has been made— (i) perform any particular act in respect of any or all the property to which the restraint order relates; (ii) take care of the said property; (iii) administer the said property; (iv) where the said property is a business or undertaking, carry on, with due regard to any law which may be applicable, the business or undertaking; and (v) in the case of property that is perishable, or liable to deterioration, decay or injury by being detained in custody, to sell or otherwise dispose of the said property; (b) order the person against whom the restraint order has been made to surrender forthwith, or within such period as that court may determine, any property in respect of which a receiver has been appointed under paragraph (a), into the custody of that receiver. (2) A person affected by an order under subsection (1)(b) may at any time apply for the variation of— (a) rescission of the order; or (b) the terms of the appointment of the manager concerned or for the discharge of that manager. (3) The court that made an order under subsection (1)(b)— (a) may at any time— (i) vary or rescind the order; or (ii) vary the terms of the appointment of the manager concerned or discharge that manager; (b) shall discharge the manager concerned if the relevant restraint order is rescinded; (c) may make an order relating to the fees and expenditure of the manager as it considers fit, including an order for the payment of the fees of the manager from the confiscated proceeds, if a confiscation order is made, or by the Government if no confiscation order is made.

sections.heading: Orders in respect of immovable property subject to restraint order

requirements.text: A court may order the Registrar of Lands to place a restriction on the land register for immovable property
The restriction ensures payment to the Government if a confiscation order has not been made
The restriction ensures payment to the Government if a confiscation order has been made but exceeds the amount payable under the order
A person affected by an order in subsection (1) may apply for rescission of the order
The court may at any time rescind the order
The court shall rescind the order if the relevant restraint order is rescinded

sections.heading: Offence may form the basis of multiple orders

sections.heading: Application of part to deceased estates

sections.source_quote: 101. Any notice authorised or required to be given to a person under this Part shall, in the case of a deceased person, be sufficiently given to the administrator of the deceased's estate. A reference in this Part to the property of a person shall, in the case of a person who is deceased, be a reference to property that the deceased held immediately before his death. sections.heading: Effect of death of joint owner of preserved property
sections.title: Effect of death on joint owned preserved property
sections.summary: Death of a joint owner after a preservation order does not transfer the interest to surviving joint owners; the preservation order continues to apply to the interest as if the person had not died. Forfeiture orders apply as if the order took effect immediately before death.
sections.dtia_summary: Preservation orders remain in effect after death of joint owner, relevant for cross-border asset transfers.

requirements.text: Death of a joint owner after preservation order does not vest interest in surviving joint owners; preservation order continues to apply as if person had not died
Forfeiture order applies as if it took effect immediately before the person died
Subsection (1) does not apply to an interest in property if preservation order ceases without forfeiture order

sections.source_quote: 102. If a person has an interest in property as a joint owner, his death after a preservation order is made in respect of the interest does not, while the order is in force, operate to vest the interest in the surviving joint owner or owners and the preservation order continues to apply to the interest as if the person had not died. A forfeiture order made in respect of that interest applies as if the order took effect in relation to the interest immediately before the person died. Subsection (1) does not apply to an interest in property if a preservation order ceases to apply to that interest without a forfeiture order being made in respect of that interest.

sections.heading: Production orders
sections.title: Production of documents for investigations
sections.summary: Police may make production orders for documents relevant to identifying, locating or quantifying property or tainted property. Police may inspect, copy, or retain documents for up to a reasonable period, and must make copies available to the person who produced them.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows production of documents for investigations, relevant for cross-border data transfers involving assets.

requirements.text: Police officer may make ex parte application with affidavit for order against person suspected of having possession or control of document relevant to identifying, locating or quantifying property
Police officer may inspect documents produced
Police officer may make copies of documents
Police officer may retain documents for as long as reasonably necessary
Police officer must make copy of documents available to person who produced them

sections.source_quote: 103. Where a person has been charged with or convicted of an offence, and a police officer has reasonable grounds for suspecting that any person has possession or control of— (a) a document relevant to identifying, locating or quantifying property of the person, or to identifying or locating a document necessary for the transfer of property of such person; or (b) a document relevant to identifying, locating or quantifying tainted property in relation to the offence, or to identifying or locating a document necessary for the transfer of tainted property in relation to the offence, the police officer may make an ex parte application with a supporting affidavit to a court for an order against the person suspected of having possession or control of a document of the kind referred to. A police officer to whom the documents are produced may— (a) inspect the documents; (b) make copies of the documents; or (c) retain the documents for as long as is reasonably necessary for the purposes of this Act. Where a police officer retains documents produced to him, he shall make a copy of the documents available to the person who produced them.
sections.relevance_reason: Police document production for financial investigations

sections.heading: Evidential value of information

sections.dtia_summary: Documents produced under orders are admissible only in specific proceedings, relevant for cross-border data transfers.

Where a person produces a document pursuant to an order under this Part, the production of the document, or any information, document or things obtained as a direct or indirect consequence of the production of the document, shall not be admissible against the person in any criminal proceedings except proceedings under section 107. sections.heading: Failure to comply with a production order
sections.title: Failure to comply with production orders for documents
sections.summary: A person commits an offence if they fail to comply with a police production order without reasonable cause or knowingly provide false/misleading documents. Conviction results in imprisonment up to seven years for individuals or fines up to two million shillings, or both, for natural persons. Corporations face fines up to ten million shillings upon conviction.

requirements.text: A person commits an offence if they fail to comply with a police production order without reasonable cause
A person commits an offence if they knowingly produce false or misleading documents
Individuals face imprisonment up to seven years or fines up to two million shillings
Corporations face fines up to ten million shillings upon conviction

sections.source_quote: 105. Failure to comply with a production order (1) Where a person is required by a production order to produce a document to a police officer, the person shall commit an offence under this section if he— (a) contravenes the order without reasonable cause; or (b) knowingly produces or makes available a document known to the person to be false or misleading in a material particular.
sections.relevance_reason: Penalties for non-compliance with data production orders

sections.heading: Power to search for and seize documents relevant to locating property

Power to search for and seize documents relevant to locating property (1) A police officer may, under warrant issued under section 107— (a) enter upon land or into premises; (b) search the land or premises for any document of the type described in section 103(1); and (c) seize any document found in the course of that search that the police officer believes, on reasonable grounds, to be a relevant document in relation to an offence, provided that the entry, search and seizure is made.

sections.heading: Search warrant for location of documents relevant to locating property

sections.heading: Searches conducted without written reasons

sections.source_quote: 108. Searches conducted without written reasons An officer or any authority exercising powers under this Act or Regulations made thereunder who, without reasons recorded in writing— (a) searches or causes to be searched any building or place; or (b) detains or searches or arrests any person, commits an offence and is liable upon conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding five years, or a fine not exceeding one million shillings, or both.

sections.heading: Finances of the Fund

sections.source_quote: 110. requirements.text: No person shall disclose information obtained during duties under this Act except for performance of duties or court-ordered actions
Persons with disclosed information must not share it with others

sections.source_quote: (1) Except for the purpose of the performance of his duties or the exercise of his functions under this Act or when lawfully required to do so by any court or under the provisions of any written law, no person shall disclose any information or matter which has been obtained by him in the performance of his duties or the exercise of his functions under this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border data transfer restrictions

sections.heading: Supersession
sections.title: Supersession of conflicting laws
sections.summary: This Act prevails over any written law in cases of conflict regarding money laundering and proceeds of crime matters. This ensures the Act's provisions are the primary legal framework.
sections.dtia_summary: Relevant for DTIA as it establishes the hierarchy of laws affecting cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: Provisions of this Act prevail over any written law in cases of conflict

sections.source_quote: Where there is a conflict between the provisions of this Act and the provisions of any written law with regard to any matter, the provisions of this Act shall prevail.
sections.relevance_reason: Preempts conflicting laws for cross-border money laundering

sections.heading: Amendment of Schedules
sections.title: Amendment of Schedules by Cabinet Secretary
sections.summary: The Cabinet Secretary can amend the First, Second, or Fourth Schedules through a Gazette order. Amended schedules cannot reduce monetary amounts specified in the Second and Fourth Schedules. This section establishes the authority and constraints for modifying regulatory schedules without diminishing financial thresholds.
sections.dtia_summary: Amendments to financial thresholds and reporting schedules must not reduce monetary values for transactions, impacting cross-border financial reporting thresholds and data collection requirements.

requirements.text: Cabinet Secretary may amend First, Second, or Fourth Schedules via Gazette order
Amended schedules cannot decrease monetary sums in Second and Fourth Schedules

sections.source_quote: 132. Amendment of Schedules (1) The Cabinet Secretary may, by order in the Gazette, amend the First, Second or the Fourth Schedules. (2) An Order made under subsection (1) shall not decrease the monetary sums specified in the Second and Fourth Schedules.
sections.relevance_reason: Regulatory amendments with financial thresholds

sections.heading: Regulations
sections.title: Regulations for Reporting and Due Diligence
sections.summary: The Cabinet Secretary must make regulations covering information reporting, client designation, legal costs, high-risk customers, and other matters. Regulations must require reporting institutions to verify customer identities, maintain transaction records, report suspicious activities, and implement internal anti-money laundering compliance programs. These rules apply to financial institutions handling money laundering risks.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulations mandate customer due diligence, transaction monitoring, and suspicious activity reporting, directly affecting data transfer and processing for financial institutions across borders.

requirements.text: Regulations must cover information reporting for money laundering
Regulations must designate persons for money laundering purposes
Regulations must prescribe maximum legal costs for property orders
Regulations must provide high-risk customer frameworks
Regulations must require customer identity verification through official records
Regulations must mandate transaction record maintenance
Regulations must require suspicious activity reporting
Regulations must establish internal reporting procedures for money laundering

sections.source_quote: 134. Regulations (1) The Cabinet Secretary shall make Regulations— (a) with regard to the nature of the information contemplated in section 44 and the manner in which it is to be reported; (b) with regard to the designation of persons for purposes of section 47; (c) prescribing maximum allowable costs for legal services in connection with an application for a preservation of property order or forfeiture order; (d) providing for high risk customers or clients; (e) providing for any matter which he may consider necessary or expedient to prescribe or to regulate in order to achieve the objects of this Act.
sections.relevance_reason: AML/KYC financial data handling

sections.heading: The Board or Advisory Board to meet at least four times in a year
sections.title: Extradition Act amendment for money laundering offences
sections.summary: This section amends the Extradition (Contiguous and Foreign Countries) Act by adding money laundering offences under the Proceeds of Crime and Anti-Money Laundering Act (2009) to the list of offences subject to extradition. This is a procedural amendment to the extradition framework.

requirements.text: The Extradition (Contiguous and Foreign Countries) Act (Cap. 76) is amended to include money laundering offences under the Proceeds of Crime and Anti-Money Laundering Act, 2009

sections.source_quote: 1. Extradition (Contiguous and Foreign Countries) Act (Cap. 76) 1.(1) This paragraph amends the Extradition (Contiguous and Foreign Countries Act (Cap. 76). (2) The schedule to the Act is amended by inserting at the end the following paragraph: "31. any offence that constitutes an offence of money laundering under the Proceeds of Crime and Anti-Money Laundering Act, 2009."

sections.title: Amendment to Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act
sections.summary: This section amends the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances (Control) Act of 1994 by repealing Section 49 of that Act. The amendment does not introduce new obligations but removes an existing provision from the controlled substances legislation.

requirements.text: Repeal Section 49 of the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances (Control) Act (of 1994)

sections.source_quote: This paragraph amends the Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances (Control) Act (of 1994). Section 49 of the Act is repealed.
